content.problemStatement:
There were NNN lottery tickets with numbers AiA_iAi‚Äč. Each number was distinct. Chef bought the first ticket, i.e. A1A_1A1‚Äč.

He is now wondering whether he won the lottery. Here is how the winner is decided:


A number XXX is decided in the range [1,106][1, 10^{6}][1,106], and then whoever has the closest ticket to XXX wins the lottery. If there is a tie, all of them are winners.
How many numbers XXX exist such that Chef won the lottery?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains NNN - the number of sold tickets
The second line of each test case contains NNN integers - A1,A2,...,ANA_1, A_2, ..., A_NA1‚Äč,A2‚Äč,...,AN‚Äč.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of integers XXX such that Chef wins the lottery.
constraints: Ai‚Č†AjA_i \ne A_jAi‚ÄčÓÄ†=Aj‚Äč for all i‚Č†ji \ne jiÓÄ†=j
The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2‚čÖ1052 \cdot 10^52‚čÖ105

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1 : The valid numbers XXX are 3,4,5,6,73, 4, 5, 6, 73,4,5,6,7 and 888. Note that 333 is winning despite it being a tie between Chef and the person who bought the 2nd2^{nd}2nd ticket, because in case of a tie all are declared winners.
Test Case 2 : All X‚Č•9X \ge 9X‚Č•9 is winning. Since we are told to count in the range [1,106][1, 10^6][1,106], the answer is all XXX in [9,106][9, 10^6][9,106] which is 999992999992999992.
